title: RFP 1212026 — 88/126
text: Blank for Photocopying ABOUT SAN DIEGO LAFCO What is LAFCO? Local Agency Formation Commissions – or LAFCOs – are boundary makers that oversee the creation and expansion of cities and special districts in all 58 counties of California.

title: RFP 1212026 — 89/126
text: and special districts in all 58 counties of California. LAFCOs exercise regulatory and planning powers to coordinate publicly provided services, such as water or fi re protection, and match them with community needs. The goal of every LAFCO is to facilitate smart growth while avoiding urban sprawl. Why were LAFCOs formed?

title: RFP 1212026 — 90/126
text: growth while avoiding urban sprawl. Why were LAFCOs formed? LAFCOs were established in 1963 by the California Legislature. Initial tasks included approving or disapproving proposals for the incorporation of new cities and creation of new special districts in 57 of the 58 counties in California.

title: RFP 1212026 — 91/126
text: special districts in 57 of the 58 counties in California. (San Francisco City/County did not have a LAFCO until 2001.

title: RFP 1212026 — 92/126
text: (San Francisco City/County did not have a LAFCO until 2001. ) The motivation leading to the establishment of LAFCOs began several years earlier when the newly elected Governor Edmund (Pat) Brown created the Commission on Metropolitan Area Problems and directed this blue-ribbon group to make recommendations to coordinate the management of statewide growth and development.

title: RFP 1212026 — 93/126
text: the management of statewide growth and development. At the time, county governments in California were largely empowered to form and expand cities and special districts without any real oversight. The prior decade highlighted the need for coordinated management as California’s population increased between 1950 and 1960 by 50 percent – from 10.

title: RFP 1212026 — 94/126
text: increased between 1950 and 1960 by 50 percent – from 10. 6 million to 15. 9 million – and more than 100 cities and special districts were formed, often with irrational and overlapping boundaries.

title: RFP 1212026 — 95/126
text: formed, often with irrational and overlapping boundaries. The Commission on Metropolitan Area Problems and its fi ndings led to a unique comprise between the State and local government with respect to assigning regional growth management responsibilities in California. This compromise remains largely intact today.

title: RFP 1212026 — 99/126
text: through various planning activities What does LAFCO do? San Diego LAFCO is a subdivision of the State of California and is responsible for administering a section of State law currently known as the Cortese-Knox-Hertzberg Local Government Reorganization Act of 2000.

title: RFP 1212026 — 100/126
text: Local Government Reorganization Act of 2000. The statute delegates the authority to regulate and plan the location of cities and most special districts and their municipal service areas to LAFCOs in all 58 counties in California.

title: RFP 1212026 — 101/126
text: service areas to LAFCOs in all 58 counties in California. This includes establishing, expanding, and reorganizing cities and special districts in meeting LAFCOs’ underlying directive from the State to facilitate smart and sustainable growth and development.

text: 92101 c/o Walt Bishop, Government Affairs wbishop@sandiego. gov SUBJECT: Response to City of San Diego’s Letter of Objection | Certificate of Sufficiency for the Proposed Special Reorganization to Incorporate the City of La Jolla (File No.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 6/94
text: to Incorporate the City of La Jolla (File No. SRO24-15) Honorable Mayor Gloria: Thank you for your letter dated April 30th regarding my prior action to issue a Certificate of Sufficiency for the above-referenced proposal on file with the San Diego County Local Agency Formation Commission (LAFCO).

title: 5225LetterMayor — 7/94
text: San Diego County Local Agency Formation Commission (LAFCO). I understand your interest in what is, by all measures, an uncommon and complex proposal under LAFCO statute — one involving the detachment of a substantial portion of the City of San Diego.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 8/94
text: of a substantial portion of the City of San Diego. Nonetheless, and as detailed below, I want to assure you that the concerns outlined in your letter are readily addressable within the framework of LAFCO statute. With this context in mind, the following responses correspond to the comments raised in sequence within your April 30 th letter.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 9/94
text: comments raised in sequence within your April 30 th letter. • With respect to Background Facts, the summarization states “LAFCO overruled the ROV’s rejection on 239 signatories” (paragraph three, page two).

title: 5225LetterMayor — 10/94
text: rejection on 239 signatories” (paragraph three, page two). This characterization – which is repeated within the letter – is inaccurate and appears to reflect a misunderstanding of the distinct responsibilities assigned to the ROV and LAFCO in reviewing voter petitions for jurisdictional changes.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 11/94
text: in reviewing voter petitions for jurisdictional changes. A proper application of these statutory distinctions makes clear LAFCO did not overrule the ROV; rather, it followed sequenced procedures and exercised San Diego LAFCO May 2, 2025 Honorable Mayor Todd Gloria of the City of San Diego Response to April 30 th Letter Regarding Certificate of Sufficiency for La Jolla Special Reorganization 2 | P a g e lawful discretion expressly delegated by the Legislature.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 12/94
text: e lawful discretion expressly delegated by the Legislature. Specifically, as outlined in Government Code Section 56707, LAFCO initially requested the ROV to the review of the petition to establish both the number of valid signatures required to meet the 25% threshold of registered voters in the affected territory and the number of qualified signatures submitted.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 13/94
text: territory and the number of qualified signatures submitted. However, Government Code Section 56706 clearly assigns to the LAFCO Executive Officer the sole authority to determine the petition’s overall validity — including whether to issue a Certificate of Sufficiency or Insufficiency.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 14/94
text: to issue a Certificate of Sufficiency or Insufficiency. In this case, the decision to issue a Certificate of Sufficiency on April 29 th followed the proponents lawful exercise of their right to challenge 240 signatories that had been initially disqualified by the ROV for reasons other than having a matching signature on file (emphasis).

title: 5225LetterMayor — 15/94
text: other than having a matching signature on file (emphasis). 1 Upon review, and as further detailed, those contested signatories were validated under the Executive Officer’s assigned responsibility and based on the criteria established in Government Code Section 56704(b), which states: “If a petition is signed by registered voters, each person signing the petition shall, in addition to his or her signature, affix the date upon which he or she signs the petition and indicate on the petition his or her place of residence, giving street and number or other designation sufficient to enable the place of residence to be readily ascertained.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 16/94
text: to enable the place of residence to be readily ascertained. ” • With respect to City Objections, the letter outlines three reasons to dispute the decision to issue the proponents a Certificate of Sufficiency.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 17/94
text: to issue the proponents a Certificate of Sufficiency. The first dispute is outlined at the bottom of page two and asserts the LAFCO Executive Officer lacks authority to conduct a secondary review of signatures once they have been reviewed by the ROV. This assertion is incorrect.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 18/94
text: have been reviewed by the ROV. This assertion is incorrect. As explained in the preceding section, the Executive Officer is explicitly assigned statutory responsibility to review and validate voter petitions for jurisdictional changes.

title: 5225LetterMayor — 19/94
